## Learning Rewards: the Canonical Paradigms {.nav-intro}

<!-- **Inverse reinforcement learning** (IRL) learns a reward function consistent with provided demonstrations or other forms of feedback -->

<!-- ::: {.callout-tip title="Definition: Markov Decision Process (MDP)"}
The MDP is the tuple $M = \langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{T}, \gamma, \mathcal{D}, R\rangle$, with states $s \in \mathcal{S}$, actions $a \in \mathcal{A}$, transitions $\mathcal{T}$, discount factor $\gamma$, initial state distribution $\mathcal{D}, and reward $R$. The MDP represents a way to model agents that are optimal to reward $R$ in their world specified by $M$.

[@irl, @maxent]{style="font-size: 0.6em;"}.
::: -->
*How do we learn rewards from human data?*

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="Definition: Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL)"}
IRL *recovers* the reward, $R$, that explains provided observations or demonstrations.

<!-- **Assumption**: IRL methods often assume provided demonstrations are [approximately optimal]{.underline} with respect to the underlying reward $R$ -->

[@irl, @maxent]{style="font-size: 0.6em;"}.
:::
:::

<!-- IRL methods often assume provided demonstrations are [approximately optimal]{.underline} with respect to the underlying reward $R$. -->

<div style="display: flex; gap: 1em;">
<div style="flex: 1;">
::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="Definition: Preference-Based Learning (PBL)"}
Rather than directly collect demonstrations, PBL infers a reward $R$ from human preferences over pairs or sets of trajectories, actions, or outcomes.

<!-- **Assumption**: PBL often does this by assuming that the provided preferences are approximately consistent to $R$. -->

[@rlhf]{style="font-size: 0.6em;"}.
:::

Notably, PBL is less query efficient than IRL.
:::
</div>
<div style="flex: 1;">
::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="Definition: Active Preference Learning (APL)"}
APL enables the learner to *actively* select which preference queries to ask the human via the *acquisition function*.

Examples: regret minimization, discriminability, information gain, and volume removal.

[@apl, @easy]{style="font-size: 0.6em;"}.
:::
:::
</div>
</div>


<!-- However, human feedback is **imperfect**.

- Inconsistency, suboptimality, and heterogeneity bias provided feedback.
- Humans are not computers: they have finite energy and are risk-averse.

Even pairwise comparisons, can vary over trials and individuals. [@exopbl]{style="font-size: 0.6em;"} -->
